skill.name: azure-data-tables-java
skill.description: Build Java applications with Azure Table Storage and Cosmos DB Table API using the Azure Tables SDK.
skill.summary: Guide Java developers through Azure Tables clients, entities, queries, transactions, and error handling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the external command and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by markdown examples and Azure terminology. The real risk is low: copied Java examples can connect to Azure services and mutate or delete table data when users provide valid credentials.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the referenced lines are markdown code fences for XML, Java, and Bash examples. No executable script, shell interpolation, or command runner is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations align with markdown fence delimiters and Java/XML/Bash documentation snippets. I found no evidence of shell command execution logic in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Azure Key Terminology Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to descriptive Azure Tables terms such as key-value data and Partition Key Design. These are database modeling concepts, not cryptographic algorithm choices.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations use the word key in Azure Tables data modeling context. No hashing, cipher, signature, or cryptographic algorithm use appears at those lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Java Accessors Flagged as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to Java getter and setter methods for entity properties. These methods do not query host identity, processes, network interfaces, or local system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are ordinary Java model accessors for partition key, row key, name, and price. I found no evidence of system information collection.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Network Access in Example Code
low_findings.description: The skill shows endpoint and credential patterns for Azure Table Storage and Cosmos DB Table API. This is expected for the skill purpose, but users should only run copied examples against approved accounts and test data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly configures Azure service endpoints and credentials. The network behavior is legitimate and scoped to Azure service usage rather than hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Data Mutation and Deletion Examples Require Care
low_findings.description: The guide includes create, update, upsert, transaction, entity deletion, and table deletion examples. These operations are normal SDK usage, but copied code can alter real cloud data when valid credentials are supplied.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples call Azure Tables SDK methods that mutate or delete entities and tables. This is expected behavior for a data SDK guide, with no evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Data Deletion Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents entity and table deletion calls. These are legitimate examples, but they are destructive if copied into production code with real credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The methods are direct delete examples. The surrounding guide presents normal SDK usage and does not hide or automate these operations.

content.user_title: Build Azure Table Storage Java Apps
content.value_statement: Java teams need correct patterns for Azure Table Storage and Cosmos DB Table API. This skill provides focused SDK examples for clients, entities, queries, transactions, and errors.
actual_capabilities: Shows the Maven dependency for the Azure Tables Java SDK.
Demonstrates client creation with connection strings, shared keys, SAS tokens, and DefaultAzureCredential.
Explains entity create, read, update, upsert, delete, and list operations.
Covers OData filters, selected properties, table listing, and batch transactions.
Includes typed entity structure and TableServiceException handling patterns.
limitations: Does not provision Azure resources or manage account permissions.
Uses brief examples instead of complete production applications.
Does not cover advanced retry, telemetry, or deployment configuration.
Includes a beta SDK version in the dependency example, which users should verify before production use.

use_cases.title: Create a Java Tables Client
use_cases.description: Set up a TableServiceClient or TableClient for an approved Azure Storage or Cosmos DB Table API account.

use_cases.title: Model and Query Table Entities
use_cases.description: Design partition keys, row keys, properties, and OData filters for a simple table-backed feature.

use_cases.title: Review SDK Usage Patterns
use_cases.description: Check CRUD, batch transaction, and error handling examples before adding Azure Tables to a service.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure Tables Java client for Azure Storage using DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic CRUD
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add create, read, update, and delete operations for a Product entity in Azure Tables Java.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Azure Tables Java query for sales records that filters by partition key and returns only needed fields.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure Tables Java approach for transactions, error handling, credentials, and partition key design.

output_examples.input: Create a client using managed identity.
output_examples.output: A short setup guide that selects DefaultAzureCredential, sets the table endpoint, and explains required Azure permissions.

output_examples.input: Plan CRUD for products stored in Azure Tables.
output: Recommended partition and row key choices.
Operations for create, merge update, read, and delete.

output_examples.output: A query review that prioritizes partition key filtering, selective projection, and smaller result sets.

best_practices: Use managed identity or scoped credentials when possible.
Design partition keys around query patterns and expected load.
Test delete and batch operations against non-production tables first.
anti_patterns: Do not paste production connection strings into prompts or source files.
Do not run delete examples against shared or production tables without review.
Do not query broad tables without partition filters when a narrower query is possible.

faq.question: Does this skill support Azure Table Storage and Cosmos DB Table API?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples cover the Azure Tables SDK patterns used with both services.

faq.question: Can it create Azure resources for me?
faq.answer: No. It explains Java SDK usage, but it does not provision accounts, tables, or permissions.

faq.question: Which authentication methods are shown?
faq.answer: It shows connection strings, shared keys, SAS tokens, and DefaultAzureCredential.

faq.question: Does it include query examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes OData filters, selected properties, result limits, and partition key guidance.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production data?
faq.answer: Use care. Some examples create, update, or delete table data when run with valid credentials.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
